fr.elements: Main Building; Roof System; Vehicle Parking Area; Pedestrian Walkways; Landscaped Area
fr.considerations: Multiple Access Points; Fenced Sections; Dense Vegetation
fr.recommendations: Ensuring multiple access points remain unobstructed and easily identifiable with appropriate signage could improve emergency responder ingress and egress. Regular maintenance of pedestrian walkways to keep them clear of obstructions and ensuring they are adequately lit may facilitate smoother emergency personnel movement. Careful management of dense vegetation near these pathways and access routes could enhance responder visibility and navigation throughout the property. Clearly demarcating vehicle parking areas and maintaining clear designation of parking spots may assist in efficient staging of emergency vehicles. Where fenced sections are present, maintaining clearly labeled and accessible gates could support effective emergency operations. Thoughtful integration of these strategies could lead to more effective emergency response capabilities across the property.

wildfire.conditions: Scattered Trees; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Vegetated Perimeter; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Open Terrain
wildfire.recommendations: Increasing the separation between the existing scattered trees and the structure could help improve defensible space. Managing the vegetation immediately adjacent to the structure by increasing the clearance and reducing fuel continuity may lower the likelihood of fire spread to the building. Additionally, evaluating and managing the vegetated perimeter to maintain open terrain could further reduce fuel continuity and support longer-term wildfire resilience. Preserving these open spaces can also create natural barriers to slow down potential fire spread. Ensuring these practices are regularly maintained will support a resilient property landscape that can facilitate firefighter access and suppression operations when necessary.
